Μετάβαση στο περιεχόμενο

Είπες:

lab1.pdf
PDF
I need yor help with Connect & Install Lab schema. I have downaloaded the XAMP and started both SQL and Apache. Now I am in step 4 where I have to open CMD

Το ChatGPT είπε:

You’re at the correct step. According to the lab slides, Step 4 says to open the Windows Command Prompt and navigate to the MariaDB/MySQL bin folder.

Here’s exactly what to do:

  1. Press Windows Key + R

  2. Type:

cmd

  1. Press Enter

A black Command Prompt window will open.

Then type the following commands one by one:

c:
cd "C:\Program Files\MariaDB 10.5\bin"

and press Enter after each line.

BUT since you installed XAMPP, your MySQL folder is probably different.

Try this instead:

cd C:\xampp\mysql\bin

or if XAMPP is installed on another drive:

cd D:\xampp\mysql\bin

The slides also mention that for your own PC you should replace the path with your actual XAMPP installation path.

After that, run:

mysql -u root -p

Then:

  • If it asks for a password, press Enter (usually blank in XAMPP unless you set one).

  • You should then see:

Welcome to the MariaDB monitor

After that you’ll be ready for the next steps:

source c:\temp\mysql-sakila-schema.sql
source c:\temp\mysql-sakila-insert-data.sql


Είπες:


Το ChatGPT είπε:

The error means your computer does not have a D: drive, so XAMPP is probably installed on C: instead.

Try these commands exactly:

cd C:\xampp\mysql\bin

Then press Enter.

If that works, continue with:

mysql -u root -p

If it still says “path not found”, check where XAMPP is installed:

  1. Open File Explorer

  2. Go to:

    • C:\xampp

    • or C:\Program Files

  3. Look for a folder named xampp

Once you find it, open:

xampp > mysql > bin

Then use that exact path in CMD.

Example:

cd C:\xampp\mysql\bin

You’re very close — this is just a folder path issue.


Είπες:

It asks for a password

Το ChatGPT είπε:

That’s normal. The command

mysql -u root -p

always asks for a password because of the -p.

In XAMPP, the default root password is usually empty.

So when you see:

Enter password:

just press Enter without typing anything.

If it works, you should see something like:

Welcome to the MariaDB monitor.

Then you can continue with the Sakila schema installation steps from the lab.


Είπες:


Το ChatGPT είπε:

That error means MySQL cannot find the .sql file in C:\temp.

You need to first download and save these two files from the Sakila GitHub page:

  • mysql-sakila-schema.sql

  • mysql-sakila-insert-data.sql

Then place them inside a folder like:

C:\temp

Your final files should exist here:

C:\temp\mysql-sakila-schema.sql
C:\temp\mysql-sakila-insert-data.sql

To check:

  1. Open File Explorer

  2. Navigate to C:\temp

  3. Verify the files are actually there

Then run again inside MariaDB:

source C:\temp\mysql-sakila-schema.sql

and after it finishes:

source C:\temp\mysql-sakila-insert-data.sql

Important:

  • Use backslashes \

  • Make sure the filename ends with .sql

  • Sometimes browsers save files as .txt accidentally, so check the extension carefully

You can download the files from:

  • Sakila GitHub Repository


Είπες:

This is my temp file

Το ChatGPT είπε:

Perfect — your files are actually in:

C:\temp\sakila-db

and the filenames are:

sakila-schema.sql
sakila-data.sql

So your previous command failed because:

  • wrong folder

  • wrong filename

Now run these commands inside MariaDB:

source C:\temp\sakila-db\sakila-schema.sql

After it finishes successfully, run:

source C:\temp\sakila-db\sakila-data.sql

If everything works, you should see many Query OK messages. Then the Sakila database will be installed.


Είπες:


Το ChatGPT είπε:

I can see the problem now: your files do not actually have the .sql extension visible in Windows.

In your folder, the files are named:

  • sakila-schema

  • sakila-data

Windows is hiding extensions, and the files may actually be:

  • sakila-schema.sql.txt

  • or another format

Do this:

  1. In File Explorer click:

    • View → Show → enable File name extensions

  2. Check the real filenames.

You need them to be exactly:

sakila-schema.sql
sakila-data.sql

If they are not:

  • Right-click → Rename

  • Add .sql

After renaming, run again:

source C:/temp/sakila-db/sakila-schema.sql

Notice I used forward slashes / — MariaDB handles them better on Windows.

Then:

source C:/temp/sakila-db/sakila-data.sql

The issue is definitely just the filename/extension now.


Είπες:

I have done what youu told me. However it still gives me the same error

Το ChatGPT είπε:

Try wrapping the path in quotes:

source "C:/temp/sakila-db/sakila-schema.sql"

If that still fails, in MariaDB type this to check what Windows sees:

system dir C:\temp\sakila-db

Look carefully at the exact filename it prints, then use that exact name.

You can also avoid the path issue completely:

exit

Then in normal CMD:

cd C:\temp\sakila-db
C:\xampp\mysql\bin\mysql -u root -p < sakila-schema.sql
C:\xampp\mysql\bin\mysql -u root -p < sakila-data.sql

When it asks for password, just press Enter.


Είπες:


Το ChatGPT είπε:

You’re missing the wildcard at the end. Run this inside MariaDB:

system dir C:\temp\sakila-db\*

But from your screenshot, Windows shows you are in temp > sakila-db, so the folder might be under your user account, not directly under C:\temp.

Try this instead:

system dir C:\Users\ntoko\temp\sakila-db\*

If that lists the files, then run:

source C:/Users/ntoko/temp/sakila-db/sakila-schema.sql

then:

source C:/Users/ntoko/temp/sakila-db/sakila-data.sql

The error means the path C:\temp\sakila-db is not the real location.


Είπες:

In SQl I have two entities: One personnel which is the father and one doctor which is the child. If I want to create a doctor table can I do it?

Το ChatGPT είπε:

Yes — absolutely. This is a common design in relational databases using inheritance/specialization.

You can have:

  • a parent table: personnel

  • a child table: doctor

where every doctor is also personnel.

Example:

SQL
CREATE TABLE personnel (
    personnel_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    first_name VARCHAR(50),
    last_name VARCHAR(50),
    salary DECIMAL(10,2)
);

Then the child table:

SQL
CREATE TABLE doctor (
    personnel_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    specialty VARCHAR(100),
    license_number VARCHAR(50),

    FOREIGN KEY (personnel_id)
        REFERENCES personnel(personnel_id)
);

How this works:

  • doctor.personnel_id is both:

    • the primary key of doctor

    • a foreign key referencing personnel

  • A doctor cannot exist unless the corresponding personnel record exists.

So to insert a doctor:

SQL
INSERT INTO personnel
VALUES (1, 'John', 'Smith', 5000);

INSERT INTO doctor
VALUES (1, 'Cardiology', 'DOC123');

This models:

Doctor IS-A Personnel

which is the correct relational approach for inheritance.


Είπες:

I haven't downloaded the sakila files from github

Το ChatGPT είπε:

That explains the whole issue — the files in your folder are not the real Sakila SQL scripts yet.

Do this:

  1. Open:
    Sakila GitHub Repository

  2. Download these two files individually:

    • mysql-sakila-schema.sql

    • mysql-sakila-insert-data.sql

For each file:

  • click the filename

  • click Raw

  • press Ctrl + S

  • save into:

C:\temp\sakila-db

  1. After downloading, your folder should contain:

C:\temp\sakila-db\mysql-sakila-schema.sql
C:\temp\sakila-db\mysql-sakila-insert-data.sql

  1. Then inside MariaDB run:

source C:/temp/sakila-db/mysql-sakila-schema.sql

and then:

source C:/temp/sakila-db/mysql-sakila-insert-data.sql

Now it should work correctly.


Είπες:

I want to create the c:\temp file first

Το ChatGPT είπε:

That’s easy.

In Windows:

  1. Open File Explorer

  2. Go to:

C:\

  1. Right-click empty space → New → Folder

  2. Name the folder:

temp

Then create another folder inside it called:

sakila-db

Final structure:

C:\temp\sakila-db

After that, download the two Sakila .sql files into that folder.


Είπες:


Το ChatGPT είπε:

You are very close now. The remaining problem is almost certainly that the file is not actually inside:

C:\temp\sakila-db

or the filename is slightly different.

Inside MariaDB, run:

system dir C:\temp\sakila-db

This time it should list the files.

You must see something like:

mysql-sakila-schema.sql
mysql-sakila-insert-data.sql

If the names appear differently, use the exact filename shown.

Then run:

source "C:/temp/sakila-db/mysql-sakila-schema.sql"

Important:

  • Use quotes " "

  • Use forward slashes /

Then:

source "C:/temp/sakila-db/mysql-sakila-insert-data.sql"

The command itself is correct now — we just need the exact filename/path to match.


Είπες:


Το ChatGPT είπε:

You accidentally started a command without finishing it, so MariaDB is waiting for more input. That’s why you see:

->

To cancel this unfinished command, type:

\c

and press Enter.

You should return to:

MariaDB [(none)]>

Then run:

system dir C:\temp\sakila-db

This will show the exact filenames inside the folder.


Είπες:


Το ChatGPT είπε:

Found it — the issue is the filenames.

Your files are:

mysql-sakila-schema.sql.txt
mysql-sakila-insert-data.sql.txt

They are saved as text files, not real .sql files.

You have two options:

Option 1 (easiest)

Use the exact filenames:

source C:/temp/sakila-db/mysql-sakila-schema.sql.txt

then:

source C:/temp/sakila-db/mysql-sakila-insert-data.sql.txt

Option 2 (better)

Rename the files in Windows:

From:

mysql-sakila-schema.sql.txt

To:

mysql-sakila-schema.sql

and:

mysql-sakila-insert-data.sql.txt

To:

mysql-sakila-insert-data.sql

Then your original source commands will work perfectly.


Είπες:

I have chosen option 1

Το ChatGPT είπε:

Great — then run these two commands exactly inside MariaDB:

source C:/temp/sakila-db/mysql-sakila-schema.sql.txt

After it completes and shows many Query OK messages, run:

source C:/temp/sakila-db/mysql-sakila-insert-data.sql.txt

If both succeed, the Sakila database is installed correctly. Then you can open:

http://localhost/phpmyadmin/

to see the database in phpMyAdmin.


Είπες:

Now what should I do in step 8?

Το ChatGPT είπε:

Step 8 is simply to verify that the Sakila database was installed correctly using phpMyAdmin.

Do this:

  1. Open your web browser

  2. Go to:

http://localhost/phpmyadmin/

  1. phpMyAdmin should open.

  2. On the left sidebar, look for a database named:

sakila

  1. Click it.

You should now see tables like:

  • actor

  • film

  • customer

  • rental

  • payment

which are part of the Sakila schema from the lab.

You can also test it by clicking the SQL tab and running:

SQL
SHOW TABLES;

or:

SQL
SELECT * FROM actor LIMIT 5;

If rows appear, everything is working correctly.


Είπες:

What is the SQL tab?

Το ChatGPT είπε:

In phpMyAdmin, the SQL tab is where you write and execute SQL commands manually.

After opening:

http://localhost/phpmyadmin/

and clicking the sakila database on the left side:

  1. Look at the top menu bar

  2. You will see tabs like:

    • Structure

    • SQL

    • Search

    • Query

    • Export

    • Import

  3. Click:

SQL

A large text box will appear where you can type SQL queries.

Example:

SQL
SHOW TABLES;

Then press the Go button.

You should see all Sakila tables listed.


Είπες:

Πως γινεται να μη ν αποθηκευω τα Queries αυτα ?

Το ChatGPT είπε:

Στο phpMyAdmin τα queries δεν αποθηκεύονται μόνιμα στη βάση εκτός αν κάνουν αλλαγές στα δεδομένα.

Υπάρχουν 2 περιπτώσεις:

  • Queries όπως:

SQL
SELECT * FROM actor;
SHOW TABLES;

μόνο εμφανίζουν δεδομένα και δεν αποθηκεύουν τίποτα.

  • Queries όπως:

SQL
INSERT INTO actor ...
UPDATE actor ...
DELETE FROM actor ...
CREATE TABLE ...

κάνουν αλλαγές και αυτές αποθηκεύονται.

Αν θες να δοκιμάζεις queries χωρίς να κρατιούνται οι αλλαγές, μπορείς να χρησιμοποιήσεις:

SQL
ROLLBACK;

ή να ξεκινήσεις transaction:

SQL
START TRANSACTION;

κάνεις δοκιμές και μετά:

SQL
ROLLBACK;

οπότε ακυρώνονται όλες οι αλλαγές.

Για απλό testing στο εργαστήριο συνήθως χρησιμοποιείτε κυρίως SELECT, άρα δεν χρειάζεται να ανησυχείς.


Είπες:

εξαμηνιαία εργασία ΒΔ 2025-2026 - v2 (3).pdf
PDF
Where does lab 1 fit in this project?

Το ChatGPT είπε:

Lab 1 is mainly the setup and familiarization phase for your semester project.

It helps you:

  1. Install the DBMS (MariaDB/MySQL)

  2. Learn how to:

    • connect to the database

    • run SQL scripts

    • use phpMyAdmin

    • execute SQL queries

  3. Understand how a relational schema works using the Sakila example database.

Your actual semester project is completely different:

  • you must design a Hospital Database System from scratch.

So Lab 1 is basically:

“Learn the tools and workflow before building your own database.”

The project itself requires you to:

  • design an ER diagram

  • convert it to relational tables

  • create the schema in SQL

  • insert data

  • create constraints and indexes

  • write 15 complex SQL queries.

The Sakila database is only an example/demo database used for practice.

It is NOT part of your hospital project.

However, Lab 1 teaches the exact workflow you will later use:

  • CREATE TABLE

  • FOREIGN KEY

  • INSERT

  • running .sql files

  • testing queries in phpMyAdmin

For example, your idea earlier:

personnel (parent) → doctor (child)

is directly related to the project requirements because the project says:

  • doctors, nurses, administrative staff are all personnel categories.

So you are already thinking in the correct direction for the actual design.


Είπες:

So I am done wiht lab 1?

Το ChatGPT είπε:

Yes — based on everything you completed, you are essentially done with Lab 1.

You have successfully:

  • Installed XAMPP

  • Started Apache and MySQL

  • Connected to MariaDB/MySQL through CMD

  • Installed the Sakila schema

  • Opened phpMyAdmin

  • Learned how to run SQL queries

Those are exactly the goals of the lab installation session.

To fully confirm, just make sure:

  1. http://localhost/phpmyadmin/ opens

  2. You can see the sakila database

  3. Queries like:

SQL
SHOW TABLES;

work successfully.

If yes → Lab 1 is complete.

Now the next important thing is starting the actual semester project:

  • ER diagram

  • relational schema

  • SQL tables

  • constraints

  • queries for the hospital database.


Είπες:

So the role of this lab is to help us visualiza our base when we have finished it?

Το ChatGPT είπε:

Partly yes — but its main role is broader than visualization.

Lab 1 is meant to teach you the whole database workflow before you build your own project.

Specifically, it teaches you how to:

  • install and run a DBMS

  • connect to MariaDB/MySQL

  • execute SQL scripts

  • use phpMyAdmin

  • inspect tables and relations

  • run SQL queries

  • understand how a relational schema is organized

The Sakila database is basically a “finished example project” that lets you explore:

  • tables

  • primary/foreign keys

  • relationships

  • realistic data

So yes, one purpose is to help you visualize how a complete database looks in practice, but also:

how to implement, load, query, and manage one.

This becomes useful for your hospital project because later you will:

  1. Design your own ER diagram

  2. Create your own tables

  3. Load your own data

  4. Run your own queries exactly like you did with Sakila

For example:

  • Sakila has film, actor, customer

  • Your project will have patient, doctor, hospitalization, department, etc.

Same relational logic, different domain.


Είπες:

lab2.pdf
PDF
Lets analytically see this lab

Το ChatGPT είπε:

Analytically, Lab 1 + Lab 2 together are building the exact mental model you need for the semester project.

Here’s the real structure behind the labs:

1. What Lab 1 actually teaches

Lab 1 is not “SQL theory”.

It teaches the environment and ecosystem:

  • DBMS installation

  • MariaDB/MySQL usage

  • phpMyAdmin

  • command line

  • importing schemas

  • exploring an existing database

  • understanding relational structure visually

The Sakila database is used as a realistic example database.

You learned:

  • tables

  • relationships

  • foreign keys

  • schema navigation

  • how a complete database looks

This is the “operating system” phase of the course.

2. What Lab 2 teaches

Lab 2 transitions from:

“How databases exist”

to:

“How we communicate with databases.”

This is the SQL language introduction.

3. The hidden architecture of SQL

Lab 2 separates SQL into 4 conceptual layers:

A. DDL — Structure

Defines the database itself.

Examples:

SQL
CREATE TABLE
ALTER TABLE
DROP TABLE

This is where your hospital schema belongs.

Example:

SQL
CREATE TABLE doctor (...)

B. DML — Data manipulation

Manipulates rows.

Examples:

SQL
INSERT
UPDATE
DELETE
SELECT

This is daily interaction with data.

C. DCL — Permissions

Security/access.

Usually less important in the early project phase.

D. TCL — Transactions

Reliability and rollback.

Examples:

SQL
COMMIT
ROLLBACK

Critical in real hospital systems.

4. The course progression

The labs are building layers:

StageWhat you learn
Lab 1Install and explore DB systems
Lab 2Basic SQL querying
Later LabsJoins, aggregation, normalization, optimization
Semester ProjectFull database engineering

5. Why Sakila matters pedagogically

Sakila is intentionally designed to resemble real systems.

For example:

SakilaHospital Project
actordoctor
customerpatient
filmtreatment/procedure
rentalhospitalization
paymentbilling
categorydepartment/specialty

The point is not movies.

The point is:

relational thinking.

6. The most important idea in Lab 2

The course is teaching that SQL is declarative.

Meaning:

You say:

SQL
SELECT patients with allergies

NOT:

loop through records manually

You describe:

WHAT you want

not:

HOW to compute it

That is the philosophical core of SQL.

7. Why SELECT is central

Most of Lab 2 focuses on SELECT.

Because almost the entire semester project’s Part B is:

advanced querying.

The 15 required queries depend heavily on:

  • SELECT

  • WHERE

  • GROUP BY

  • HAVING

  • JOIN

  • ORDER BY

  • aggregation

Exactly what Lab 2 introduces.

8. The deeper conceptual jump

The course is gradually transforming your thinking from:

Programming mindset:
objects + loops

to:

Relational mindset:
entities + relations + constraints + set operations

That’s why the professor discourages ORM-heavy thinking.

They want you to think:

  • relationally

  • mathematically

  • declaratively

not procedurally.

9. How your project fits now

Your project now becomes:

Step 1

Extract entities from the description:

  • personnel

  • doctor

  • nurse

  • patient

  • hospitalization

  • department

  • shift

  • medication

  • surgery
    etc.

Step 2

Define relationships:

  • doctor supervises doctor

  • doctor belongs to department

  • patient has hospitalization

  • hospitalization uses bed

Step 3

Transform into relational tables.

Step 4

Implement with DDL:

SQL
CREATE TABLE ...

Step 5

Load data.

Step 6

Answer analytical SQL queries.

10. The real purpose of the labs

The labs are not isolated exercises.

They are:

incremental scaffolding toward building a complete relational information system.


Είπες:


Το ChatGPT είπε:

Perfect. This screenshot actually represents an important conceptual transition.

You just did:

SQL
SHOW DATABASES;

which asks:

“What databases exist inside the DBMS?”

Then:

SQL
USE sakila;

which means:

“Set sakila as the active working database.”

This is analogous to:

ConceptAnalogy
DBMSOperating System
DatabaseProject Folder
TableFile
RowRecord
QuerySearch/operation

So now your “workspace” is the sakila database.

That’s why the prompt changed from:

MariaDB [(none)]>

to:

MariaDB [sakila]>

This means:

  • before: no database selected

  • now: all queries operate inside sakila

This is the exact same workflow you’ll later use for your project.

Eventually you’ll do something like:

SQL
CREATE DATABASE hospital_db;
USE hospital_db;

and then create your own tables.

Now that you are inside sakila, you can start the real exploration phase from Lab 2:

See the tables

SQL
SHOW TABLES;

Inspect a table structure

SQL
DESC actor;

Read data

SQL
SELECT * FROM actor LIMIT 5;

This sequence is fundamental relational exploration:

Database
    ↓
Tables
    ↓
Columns
    ↓
Rows

That hierarchy is the core mental model of relational databases.


Είπες:


Το ChatGPT είπε:

This slide is introducing the PostgreSQL command-line client (psql) and comparing it to the MariaDB/MySQL client you already used.

Conceptually, the important point is:

Every DBMS has:

  • a database engine

  • a command-line client

  • a query language (SQL)

The SQL language is mostly standardized, but the tools differ.

What you already used

You used:

mysql -u root -p

which opened the MariaDB client:

MariaDB [sakila]>

What this slide shows

This slide shows PostgreSQL’s equivalent client:

psql.exe user=postgres

which opens:

postgres=#

Same idea:

  • connect to DBMS

  • choose database

  • execute SQL

Different software.

Why the encoding command exists

This line:

chcp 1253

changes the Windows terminal encoding to Greek Windows encoding.

Why?

Because PostgreSQL on Windows CMD may display Greek characters incorrectly otherwise.

Encoding is about:

how bytes become visible characters.

Compare the prompts

DBMSPrompt
MariaDB/MySQLMariaDB [sakila]>
PostgreSQLpostgres=#

The prompt tells you:

  • which DBMS you are connected to

  • which database is active

  • whether you are admin/root

The important abstraction

The course is subtly teaching:

SQL is portable.
Tools are replaceable.

Meaning:

  • relational theory stays mostly the same

  • syntax changes slightly

  • commands/tools differ

  • concepts remain identical

MariaDB vs PostgreSQL mentally

MariaDB/MySQLPostgreSQL
SHOW DATABASES;\l
USE sakila;\c sakila
SHOW TABLES;\dt
DESC actor;\d actor

But:

SQL
SELECT * FROM actor;

works almost identically in both.

Why the professor shows both

The course allows:

  • MariaDB/MySQL

  • PostgreSQL

So the labs demonstrate:

  • both environments

  • both clients

  • equivalent workflows

Important insight

Notice something subtle:

Commands like:

\dt
\d actor
\l

are NOT SQL.

They are:

client commands (psql meta-commands)

Whereas:

SQL
SELECT * FROM actor;

IS SQL.

This distinction becomes important later.

Big picture

This slide is not really about PostgreSQL.

It is teaching:

  1. DBMS clients exist

  2. SQL is executed through clients

  3. Different systems expose the same relational concepts differently

  4. SQL itself is the stable abstraction layer

That’s the deeper lesson.


Είπες:

?

Το ChatGPT είπε:

This slide demonstrates the lifecycle of creating and activating a database.

Let’s analyze it carefully.

1. CREATE DATABASE

SQL
CREATE DATABASE test1;

This is a DDL command (Data Definition Language).

It tells the DBMS:

“Create a new logical container named test1.”

Conceptually:

DBMS
 ├── sakila
 ├── mysql
 ├── phpmyadmin
 └── test1   ← new empty database

At this point:

  • no tables exist

  • no rows exist

  • only the database container exists

2. USE test1

SQL
USE test1;

This changes the active working database.

Before:

MariaDB [(none)]>

After:

MariaDB [test1]>

Meaning:

“All subsequent SQL statements operate inside test1.”

3. SHOW CREATE DATABASE

SQL
SHOW CREATE DATABASE test1;

This is introspection.

You are asking MariaDB:

“Show me the exact internal SQL definition used to create this database.”

The DBMS responds with:

SQL
CREATE DATABASE `test1`
DEFAULT CHARACTER SET utf8mb4

4. Why this matters conceptually

This reveals a powerful idea:

SQL is self-describing.

The DBMS stores:

  • schema definitions

  • metadata

  • constraints

  • database structure

inside itself.

This is called:

the system catalog / data dictionary.

5. Character set (utf8mb4)

This part:

DEFAULT CHARACTER SET utf8mb4

is extremely important.

It defines:

how text is encoded and stored.

utf8mb4 supports:

  • English

  • Greek

  • emojis

  • multilingual data

Without correct encoding:

  • Greek text may break

  • accents may corrupt

  • symbols may fail

For your hospital project, UTF-8 support is essential because:

  • patient names

  • diagnoses

  • Greek medical terms
    must store correctly.

6. Hidden philosophical idea

This slide introduces the distinction between:

LayerMeaning
DatabaseLogical container
TableStructured relation
RowInstance/data
ColumnAttribute/type

You are still at the:

database container layer.

Tables come later.

7. What you will eventually do

For your project, you will likely create:

SQL
CREATE DATABASE hospital_db;
USE hospital_db;

Then define tables:

SQL
CREATE TABLE personnel (...);
CREATE TABLE doctor (...);
CREATE TABLE patient (...);

So this slide is teaching the very first structural step of database engineering.

8. Important mental distinction

Many beginners confuse:

ThingMeaning
Databasecontainer/project
Tableentity/relation
Rowactual data
Queryoperation

This slide focuses ONLY on:

creating the container.

Not the schema yet.

9. Deeper interpretation

A relational DBMS behaves almost like:

  • a filesystem

  • plus a compiler

  • plus a query engine

  • plus a constraint system

CREATE DATABASE
is analogous to:

mkdir project_folder

while:

SQL
CREATE TABLE

is analogous to:

creating structured files/types

That’s the abstraction being introduced here.


Είπες:

Do I write here or do I enter Maria DB?

Το ChatGPT είπε:

You are currently in normal Windows CMD inside the folder:

C:\xampp\mysql\bin>

To execute SQL commands like:

SQL
CREATE DATABASE test1;

you must first enter the MariaDB client.

So now type:

mysql -u root -p

Press Enter.

When it asks for password:

  • just press Enter again (if you didn’t set one)

Then you should see something like:

MariaDB [(none)]>

NOW you can write SQL commands such as:

SQL
CREATE DATABASE test1;
USE test1;
SHOW DATABASES;

Important distinction:

LocationWhat you can do
C:\xampp\mysql\bin>Windows commands
MariaDB [(none)]>SQL commands

So:

  • CMD launches programs

  • MariaDB executes SQL


Είπες:


Το ChatGPT είπε:

This slide is extremely important because it introduces:

database schema automation through SQL scripts.

This is exactly how real database projects are built.

What the slide is doing

The file contains:

SQL
CREATE DATABASE movies1;
USE movies1;

CREATE TABLE movies(
    title VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    genre VARCHAR(30) NOT NULL,
    director VARCHAR(60) NOT NULL,
    release_year INT NOT NULL,
    PRIMARY KEY(title)
);

INSERT INTO movies VALUE
("Joker", "psychological thriller", "Todd Phillips", 2019);

This is a complete database setup script.

Conceptual decomposition

The script has 4 layers:

LayerPurpose
CREATE DATABASEcreate container
USEactivate database
CREATE TABLEdefine structure
INSERT INTOinsert actual data

This is the fundamental lifecycle of relational systems.

1. CREATE TABLE

This is the key new concept.

You are defining:

  • entity structure

  • attributes

  • types

  • constraints

Conceptually:

Entity → movies
Attributes →
    title
    genre
    director
    release_year

2. Data types

SQL
VARCHAR(50)
INT

define:

what kind of values are allowed.

This is:

domain integrity.

3. NOT NULL

SQL
title VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL

means:

the column cannot be empty.

This is a constraint.

4. PRIMARY KEY

SQL
PRIMARY KEY(title)

means:

  • every movie title must be unique

  • identifies each row uniquely

This introduces:

entity integrity.

5. INSERT INTO

SQL
INSERT INTO movies VALUE (...);

creates an actual row.

Now the table moves from:

schema

to:

data.

6. The critical engineering idea

The most important part of the slide is actually this:

save as file: myddl.sql

This teaches:

schemas should be reproducible.

Instead of manually creating tables one by one,
you store everything in SQL files.

This is exactly what your semester project requires:

sql/install.sql
sql/load.sql

7. Why run from a file?

This command:

mysql -u root -p < myddl.sql

means:

“Feed the SQL file into MariaDB automatically.”

This is called:

  • script execution

  • schema deployment

It automates:

  • database creation

  • table creation

  • inserts

8. This is exactly what Sakila did

Earlier you ran:

source mysql-sakila-schema.sql

That was the same idea:

  • execute a large SQL script

  • automatically create an entire database

So this slide explains what you already did mechanically.

9. This becomes your project architecture

Eventually your project will contain:

install.sql

SQL
CREATE TABLE personnel (...);
CREATE TABLE doctor (...);
CREATE TABLE patient (...);

load.sql

SQL
INSERT INTO patient ...
INSERT INTO doctor ...

Exactly this structure.

10. The deeper abstraction

This slide is teaching that:

databases are programmable infrastructure.

You are not just storing data.

You are:

  • defining rules

  • defining structure

  • enforcing constraints

  • automating deployment

That’s why SQL scripts are central to database engineering.


Είπες:

ENGINE=InnoDB; What is this

Το ChatGPT είπε:

ENGINE=InnoDB specifies:

which storage engine MariaDB/MySQL should use internally for the table.

Example:

SQL
CREATE TABLE doctor (
    doctor_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    name VARCHAR(50)
) ENGINE=InnoDB;

What is a storage engine?

Inside MySQL/MariaDB, tables are not stored directly by SQL itself.

A lower-level subsystem called a storage engine manages:

  • physical storage

  • indexes

  • transactions

  • locking

  • foreign keys

  • recovery

Think of it like:

SQL layer
    ↓
Storage Engine
    ↓
Disk files

Why InnoDB matters

InnoDB is the modern/default engine because it supports:

FeatureSupported?
Transactions✅
COMMIT / ROLLBACK✅
Foreign keys✅
Crash recovery✅
Row-level locking✅

This is essential for serious systems like:

  • hospitals

  • banking

  • enterprise applications

Example

Your hospital project will heavily depend on:

SQL
FOREIGN KEY (doctor_id)
REFERENCES personnel(personnel_id)

Foreign keys require InnoDB.

Historical context

Older MySQL often used:

MyISAM

which:

  • was faster for simple reads

  • BUT lacked:

    • transactions

    • foreign keys

    • reliability

So modern systems almost always use:

InnoDB

Why this matters conceptually

ENGINE=InnoDB separates:

LayerResponsibility
SQLlogical structure
InnoDBphysical implementation

SQL says:

SQL
CREATE TABLE patient

InnoDB decides:

  • how rows are stored

  • how indexes work

  • how concurrency is handled

  • how rollback is implemented

Transaction example

Because of InnoDB, this works:

SQL
START TRANSACTION;

UPDATE account
SET balance = balance - 100
WHERE id = 1;

ROLLBACK;

Without transaction support:

  • money could disappear

  • data corruption could occur

In a hospital system, that would be catastrophic.

For your project

You usually won’t need to specify it manually because:

  • MariaDB defaults to InnoDB nowadays

But understanding it is important because:

your relational constraints rely on it internally.

Especially:

  • foreign keys

  • consistency

  • rollback

  • concurrent access

all depend heavily on InnoDB.


Είπες:

εξαμηνιαία εργασία ΒΔ 2025-2026 - v2 (3).pdf
PDF
Αυτη ειναι η εξαμηνιαια εργασια μας στο μαθημα των βασεων. Μεχρι στιγμης εχουμε κανει το er διαγραμμα αλλα και το σχεσιακο. Ο συνεργατης μου ασχολειται με τα Constrains και τα indexes. Εγω θελω να γεμισω τους σχετικους πίνακες. Το er το εχουμε κανει στο MySQL Workbench. Θελω απο εκει να με βοηθησεις αναλυτικα με την συμπληρωση των πινακων λαμβανοντας υποψιν τους περιορισμους της ασκησης σχετικα με το πληθος των οντοτητων

Το ChatGPT είπε:

Ναι, μπορώ να σε βοηθήσω αναλυτικά. Το δικό σου κομμάτι είναι ουσιαστικά το sql/load.sql, δηλαδή το script που θα γεμίζει τη βάση με δεδομένα. Η εκφώνηση ζητά ενδεικτικά τουλάχιστον 80 ιατρούς, 200 ασθενείς, 500 νοσηλείες, 15 τμήματα, 300 συνταγογραφήσεις, 10 χειρουργεία/αίθουσες, 150 ιατρικές πράξεις και 200 εργαστηριακές εξετάσεις.

Η σωστή σειρά που πρέπει να γεμίσεις τους πίνακες είναι αυτή:

1. Πίνακες αναφοράς
   - ειδικότητες
   - βαθμίδες
   - ασφαλιστικοί φορείς
   - ICD-10
   - ΚΕΝ
   - δραστικές ουσίες
   - φάρμακα
   - τύποι εξετάσεων / πράξεων

2. Τμήματα
   - 15 departments

3. Κλίνες
   - beds ανά τμήμα

4. Προσωπικό
   - personnel
   - doctors
   - nurses
   - administrative staff

5. Συσχετίσεις προσωπικού
   - doctor_department
   - supervisors για doctors

6. Ασθενείς
   - 200 patients
   - emergency contacts
   - allergies

7. Triage περιστατικά

8. Νοσηλείες
   - 500 hospitalizations

9. Εξετάσεις
   - 200 lab exams

10. Ιατρικές πράξεις / επεμβάσεις
   - 150 medical acts/procedures
   - surgeries / rooms / assistants

11. Συνταγογραφήσεις
   - 300 prescriptions

12. Αξιολογήσεις
   - μόνο για ολοκληρωμένες νοσηλείες

Ο λόγος που αυτή η σειρά είναι σημαντική είναι τα foreign keys. Δεν μπορείς π.χ. να βάλεις νοσηλεία αν δεν υπάρχει ήδη ο ασθενής, το τμήμα και η κλίνη.

Για να σε βοηθήσω σωστά από το MySQL Workbench, χρειάζομαι ένα από αυτά:

  1. Το Forward Engineer SQL script από Workbench, δηλαδή το CREATE TABLE ... αρχείο.

  2. Ή screenshot / export του σχεσιακού διαγράμματος με όλα τα table names και columns.

  3. Ή απλώς να μου κάνεις copy-paste εδώ το CREATE TABLE για τους πίνακες σας.

Το καλύτερο είναι να μου στείλεις το αρχείο install.sql που βγάζει το MySQL Workbench. Τότε μπορώ να σου φτιάξω βήμα-βήμα το load.sql με σωστή σειρά inserts και με δεδομένα που καλύπτουν τα ζητούμενα queries.


Είπες:

Ποση ωρα πιστευεις θα μου παριε για να συμπληρωσω ολους τους πινακες?

Το ChatGPT είπε:

Εξαρτάται κυρίως από:

  • πόσο “καθαρό” είναι το schema σας,

  • αν έχετε ήδη όλα τα FK/constraints σωστά,

  • και αν θα κάνεις manual inserts ή semi-automated generation.

Με βάση την εκφώνηση, το να γεμίσεις σωστά τη βάση δεν είναι μικρό task. Δεν μιλάμε για “βάζω 10 rows”. Πρέπει τα δεδομένα:

  • να περνούν constraints,

  • να βγάζουν αποτελέσματα στα 15 queries,

  • να είναι λογικά συνεπή μεταξύ τους.

Ρεαλιστικά:

ΠροσέγγισηΕκτίμηση
Manual inserts όλα με το χέρι25–40 ώρες
Semi-automated (σωστή λύση)8–15 ώρες
Πλήρως scripted generation5–10 ώρες

Η σωστή στρατηγική ΔΕΝ είναι:

γράφω χιλιάδες INSERT manually

Θα καταστραφείτε χρονικά.

Η σωστή προσέγγιση είναι:

1. Χώρισε τους πίνακες σε κατηγορίες

A. Static/reference tables

Μικροί πίνακες:

  • departments

  • nurse grades

  • doctor ranks

  • insurance providers

  • room types

Αυτά:

  • 30–60 λεπτά total.

B. Medium entity tables

  • personnel

  • doctors

  • nurses

  • admin staff

  • patients

  • beds

Αυτά μπορούν να γίνουν:

  • με Excel,

  • Mockaroo,

  • generatedata,

  • Python script,

  • ή SQL loops/procedures.

~3–5 ώρες.

C. High-complexity relational tables

Αυτά είναι το δύσκολο μέρος:

  • hospitalizations

  • shifts

  • prescriptions

  • surgeries

  • surgery assistants

  • allergies

  • evaluations

  • triage

Γιατί;
Επειδή έχουν:

  • temporal consistency,

  • FK dependencies,

  • business rules,

  • cardinality constraints.

Εδώ θα φας τον περισσότερο χρόνο:
~5–10 ώρες.

Το μεγάλο hidden problem

Δεν αρκεί:

να γεμίσουν οι πίνακες

Πρέπει:

να δουλεύουν τα queries

Αυτό είναι το σημαντικότερο σημείο της εκφώνησης.

Παράδειγμα:

Αν δεν υπάρχουν:

  • ασθενείς με >3 νοσηλείες στο ίδιο τμήμα,
    τότε το Query 3:

δεν επιστρέφει τίποτα

και χάνετε βαθμούς.

Άρα τα δεδομένα πρέπει να είναι:

query-aware.

Αυτό που κάνουν οι περισσότεροι λάθος

Γεμίζουν:

  • random δεδομένα,
    και μετά:

  • τα queries δεν επιστρέφουν σωστά αποτελέσματα.

Εσείς πρέπει να σχεδιάσετε:

“data scenarios”.

Η στρατηγική που προτείνω

Βήμα 1

Φτιάξε πρώτα:

  • departments

  • beds

  • personnel

  • doctors

  • patients

Βήμα 2

Γράψε πρώτα ΟΛΑ τα queries.

Ναι, πριν γεμίσεις πλήρως τη βάση.

Γιατί τότε θα ξέρεις:

  • τι patterns χρειάζονται.

Παράδειγμα query-driven data

Για το Query 11:

“ιατροί που έχουν 5 λιγότερες επεμβάσεις από τον top surgeon”

χρειάζεσαι:

  • skewed distribution χειρουργείων.

Άρα:

  • ένας doctor με 40 surgeries,

  • άλλοι με 35,

  • άλλοι με 10.

Ρεαλιστική συμβουλή

ΜΗΝ προσπαθήσεις να κάνεις:

INSERT manually 500 hospitalizations

Θα είναι εφιάλτης.

Κάνε:

  • μικρά handcrafted core datasets

    • generated bulk data.

Ιδανικό workflow

MySQL Workbench

→ schema/export

Excel/CSV

→ μαζικά δεδομένα

LOAD DATA INFILE

→ import CSVs

Αυτό είναι το professional approach.

Τι θα έκανα εγώ στη θέση σας

Day 1

  • finalize schema

  • constraints

  • indexes

Day 2

  • reference tables

  • personnel

  • departments

  • patients

Day 3

  • hospitalizations

  • prescriptions

  • procedures

Day 4

  • tune data ώστε να περνάνε όλα τα queries

Day 5

  • EXPLAIN ANALYZE

  • indexes optimization

  • cleanup/report

Αν μου στείλεις το actual relational schema / install.sql, μπορώ να σου δώσω:

  • ακριβή σειρά population,

  • ποιοι πίνακες γεμίζουν manually,

  • ποιοι automated,

  • πώς να παράξεις consistent δεδομένα,

  • και πώς να στήσεις data ώστε να περνάνε όλα τα queries.


Είπες:

Αμα ξεκινησω γεμιζοντας τα ICD 10 και το KEN?

Το ChatGPT είπε:

Ναι — είναι πολύ καλή αρχή.

Τα ICD-10 και ΚΕΝ είναι “reference data”, δηλαδή πίνακες αναφοράς. Η εκφώνηση λέει ότι αυτά πρέπει να μπουν ως έχουν από τις επίσημες πηγές και δεν επιτρέπεται να εφεύρετε δικούς σας κωδικούς ή ονόματα.

Ξεκίνα με αυτή τη σειρά:

1. ICD-10
2. KEN
3. Ιατρικές πράξεις reference table
4. Φάρμακα / δραστικές ουσίες

Μετά θα μπορείς να γεμίσεις τους πιο “ζωντανούς” πίνακες:

hospitalization
lab_exam
medical_procedure
prescription

Γιατί οι νοσηλείες χρειάζονται ICD-10 και ΚΕΝ: κάθε νοσηλεία έχει διάγνωση εισαγωγής/εξόδου ICD-10 και αντιστοιχίζεται σε κωδικό ΚΕΝ για το κόστος.

Πρακτικά, για αρχή φτιάξε/γέμισε:

SQL
icd10(
  icd10_code,
  description
)

και:

SQL
ken(
  ken_code,
  description,
  base_cost,
  average_length_of_stay,
  extra_daily_cost
)

Μετά στις νοσηλείες θα έχεις foreign keys όπως:

SQL
admission_icd10_code
discharge_icd10_code
ken_code

Η καλύτερη μέθοδος είναι να τα βάλεις από CSV/Excel με LOAD DATA INFILE, όχι με χειροκίνητα INSERT, γιατί η εκφώνηση το προτείνει για μαζική εισαγωγή αρχείων αναφοράς.


Είπες:

How can I see my invites in github

Το ChatGPT είπε:

To see your GitHub invitations:

  1. Open GitHub

  2. Log in

  3. Click your profile picture (top-right)

  4. Go to:

Your organizations

or directly open:

GitHub Invitations Page

You can also check:

Repository Invitations

and scroll to:

Repository invitations

If someone invited you to:

  • a repository,

  • an organization,

  • or a classroom assignment,

you’ll see an Accept invitation button there.


Είπες:

Can you please tell me quickly how to use GitHub

Το ChatGPT είπε:

GitHub in 10 Minutes (practical version)

Think of GitHub as:

Google Drive for code/projects + version history + collaboration.

1. Core concepts

ConceptMeaning
Gitversion control system
GitHubwebsite hosting Git repositories
Repository (repo)project folder
Commitsaved snapshot
Pushupload local changes
Pulldownload latest changes
Branchseparate development line

2. Typical workflow

Create repo
    ↓
Clone locally
    ↓
Edit files
    ↓
Commit changes
    ↓
Push to GitHub

3. Install Git

Download:

Git Downloads

Install with default settings.

4. Configure Git once

Open CMD/Git Bash:

Bash
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "your@email.com"

5. Clone a repository

If your teammate invited you:

  1. Open repo on GitHub

  2. Click green Code button

  3. Copy HTTPS URL

Then:

Bash
git clone REPO_URL

Example:

Bash
git clone https://github.com/user/hospital-db.git

This downloads the project locally.

6. Open project folder

Bash
cd hospital-db

7. Add/edit files

Example:

  • install.sql

  • load.sql

  • diagrams

  • README

8. Save changes locally (commit)

Bash
git add .
git commit -m "Added ICD10 data"

Think:

commit = checkpoint/save state

9. Upload to GitHub

Bash
git push

Now teammates see your changes.

10. Before starting work ALWAYS do:

Bash
git pull

This downloads teammates’ latest updates.

Very important.

11. Minimal commands you actually need

Honestly, for your course project, 95% of the time you'll use only:

Bash
git pull
git add .
git commit -m "message"
git push

That’s enough.

12. Recommended project structure

Your assignment already specifies structure: 

project/
│
├── diagrams/
├── sql/
│   ├── install.sql
│   ├── load.sql
│   ├── Q01.sql
│   └── ...
├── docs/
├── README.md

13. Important collaboration rule

Never both edit:

same file
same lines
same time

Otherwise merge conflicts happen.

14. Easiest workflow for your team

You:

  • load.sql

  • sample data

  • CSV imports

Teammate:

  • constraints

  • indexes

  • install.sql

This minimizes conflicts.

15. Easiest GUI option

If terminal scares you, use:

GitHub Desktop

It gives buttons for:

  • commit

  • pull

  • push

  • history

Much easier for beginners.

16. The single most important thing

GitHub is NOT:

save latest version only

It is:

save evolution/history of project

Every commit is a historical snapshot.


Είπες:

this is my workspace with my teammate. I need you to teach me quickly how to use it fro our project

Το ChatGPT είπε:

Perfect — this is already enough for your semester project workflow.

You have:

  • a GitHub repository,

  • your .mwb Workbench model,

  • install.sql,

  • collaboration setup.

Now you only need a simple team workflow. Do NOT overcomplicate Git.

What your repository currently is

Your repo is basically:

shared project folder in the cloud

Everything your team uploads becomes visible to everyone.

Your current files

FileMeaning
db.mwbMySQL Workbench ER model
install.sqlschema creation script

Soon you’ll add:

load.sql
Q01.sql
Q02.sql
...
README.md

according to the assignment structure.

THE ONLY WORKFLOW YOU REALLY NEED

You basically need only 4 commands.

1. Clone the project ONCE

Click green:

Code

Copy HTTPS URL.

Then in CMD:

Bash
git clone REPO_URL

Example:

Bash
git clone https://github.com/steliosthemelis/Hospital_DB.git

This downloads the project locally.

2. Enter the project folder

Bash
cd Hospital_DB

Now this folder is synchronized with GitHub.

3. Before EVERY work session

Run:

Bash
git pull

Meaning:

“Download teammate’s latest changes.”

VERY IMPORTANT.

Always pull first.

4. After you modify files

Example:

  • updated load.sql

  • added CSV

  • changed README

Run:

Bash
git add .
git commit -m "Added ICD10 import"
git push

Meaning:

CommandMeaning
git add .prepare changes
git commitsave snapshot
git pushupload to GitHub

That’s literally enough for the whole course.

Recommended project structure

Right now your repo is flat.

Organize it EXACTLY like the assignment says: 

Hospital_DB/
│
├── diagrams/
│   ├── er.pdf
│   └── relational.pdf
│
├── sql/
│   ├── install.sql
│   ├── load.sql
│   ├── Q01.sql
│   ├── Q02.sql
│   └── ...
│
├── docs/
│   └── report.pdf
│
├── code/
│
└── README.md

Do this NOW before the repo becomes messy.

Your role specifically

Since you handle data population:

You should mainly work on:

sql/load.sql
csv/
reference data

Your teammate:

install.sql
constraints
indexes

This minimizes merge conflicts.

IMPORTANT: Never edit simultaneously

Avoid:

same file
same lines
same time

Otherwise Git conflicts happen.

Best strategy for your project

1. Work locally

In:

Hospital_DB/sql/load.sql

2. Test in MySQL

Bash
mysql -u root -p < load.sql

3. If it works:

Bash
git add .
git commit -m "Added hospitalization inserts"
git push

Done.

Super important for databases

DO NOT store:

actual database files

Store:

SQL scripts only

Meaning:
✅ good:

install.sql
load.sql
queries

❌ bad:

xampp/mysql/data/*

How you and teammate collaborate

Teammate updates schema

You:

Bash
git pull

Now you have latest schema.

You update data

You:

Bash
git push

Teammate:

Bash
git pull

The REAL mental model

GitHub is NOT:

cloud storage

It is:

history + synchronization

Every commit is:

a recoverable historical state.

Honestly, for this course:

You can survive using ONLY:

Bash
git pull
git add .
git commit -m "message"
git push

That’s enough.


Είπες:


Το ChatGPT είπε:

This means Git is not installed yet (or not added to PATH).

Do this:

  1. Download Git:

Git for Windows Download

  1. Install it with the default settings.

IMPORTANT:
During installation make sure this option is selected:

Git from the command line and also from 3rd-party software

  1. After installation:

  • close CMD

  • reopen CMD

  1. Test installation:

Bash
git --version

You should see something like:

git version 2.xx.x

  1. Then run again:

Bash
git clone https://github.com/steliosthemelis/Hospital_DB.git

After cloning:

Bash
cd Hospital_DB

Now your local folder is connected to GitHub.


Είπες:

in choosing the default editor used by Git what should I choose?

Το ChatGPT είπε:

Choose:

Visual Studio Code

IF you already have VS Code installed.

Otherwise choose:

Vim

BUT for beginners, VS Code is much easier.

The editor is only used when Git occasionally opens commit messages or merge messages.

So:

If you have VS CodeChoose
✅Visual Studio Code
If notChoose
✅Vim (default)

For your course project, either is fine because you’ll mostly use:

Bash
git commit -m "message"

which bypasses the editor anyway.


Είπες:

sql/load.sql csv/ reference data I want to start now with doing the load.sql. I need you to tell me what you need in order to provide help and I also need you to tell me ste by stel how to create the load.sql where to do it (if I can do it on VSCode I would prepfer it over cmd).

Το ChatGPT είπε:

Ναι, μπορείς να το κάνεις κανονικά στο VS Code. Το CMD θα το χρειάζεσαι μόνο για να τρέχεις/testάρεις το αρχείο.

Για να σε βοηθήσω σωστά με το load.sql, χρειάζομαι πρώτα ένα από αυτά:

  1. Το αρχείο install.sql που έχετε στο GitHub.

  2. Ή export από MySQL Workbench με όλα τα CREATE TABLE.

  3. Ή screenshots/tables με:

    • table names

    • columns

    • primary keys

    • foreign keys

Χωρίς το schema, δεν μπορώ να γράψω σωστά inserts, γιατί πρέπει να ξέρω ακριβώς ονόματα πινάκων/στηλών και τη σειρά των foreign keys.

Η εκφώνηση ζητά το παραδοτέο να έχει φάκελο sql/ με install.sql, load.sql και τα queries Qx.sql, άρα το load.sql είναι ακριβώς το σωστό αρχείο για το δικό σου κομμάτι.

Βήμα-βήμα στο VS Code

1. Άνοιξε το project folder

Στο VS Code:

File → Open Folder

και διάλεξε τον φάκελο:

Hospital_DB

2. Φτιάξε φάκελο sql

Αν δεν υπάρχει ήδη:

Right click στο project → New Folder → sql

3. Μετακίνησε το install.sql

Βάλε το υπάρχον install.sql μέσα στο:

Hospital_DB/sql/install.sql

4. Δημιούργησε το load.sql

Right click στον φάκελο sql:

New File → load.sql

Άρα θα έχεις:

Hospital_DB/
├── db.mwb
└── sql/
    ├── install.sql
    └── load.sql

5. Δημιούργησε φάκελο csv

Για μεγάλα δεδομένα:

Hospital_DB/
├── csv/
│   ├── icd10.csv
│   ├── ken.csv
│   └── ...
└── sql/
    ├── install.sql
    └── load.sql

Πώς ξεκινάς το load.sql

Στην αρχή βάλε:

SQL
USE hospital_db;

SET FOREIGN_KEY_CHECKS = 0;

-- Reference data first
-- ICD10
-- KEN
-- Departments
-- Personnel
-- Patients
-- Hospitalizations

SET FOREIGN_KEY_CHECKS = 1;

Το hospital_db πρέπει να είναι το πραγματικό όνομα της βάσης σας από το install.sql.

Πρώτη σωστή σειρά γεμίσματος

Ξεκίνα από πίνακες που δεν εξαρτώνται από άλλους:

1. ICD10
2. KEN
3. Insurance providers
4. Departments
5. Beds
6. Personnel
7. Doctors / Nurses / Admins
8. Patients
9. Hospitalizations
10. Lab exams
11. Medical procedures
12. Prescriptions
13. Evaluations

Τα δεδομένα αναφοράς όπως ICD-10, ΚΕΝ, φάρμακα και ιατρικές πράξεις πρέπει να μπουν από τις αντίστοιχες πηγές και όχι να τα εφεύρετε.

Πώς θα το testάρεις

Από CMD, μέσα στο project:

Bash
mysql -u root -p < sql/install.sql
mysql -u root -p < sql/load.sql

Αν δεν έχεις password, πατάς απλά Enter.

Τι να μου στείλεις τώρα

Στείλε μου το περιεχόμενο του install.sql ή ανέβασέ το εδώ, και θα σου φτιάξω ακριβώς:

1. σωστή σειρά population
2. skeleton load.sql
3. ποιοι πίνακες γεμίζουν με INSERT
4. ποιοι καλύτερα με CSV/LOAD DATA
5. πόσα rows χρειάζεται κάθε βασικός πίνακας

Είπες:

Where is the file Hospital_DB?

Το ChatGPT είπε:

Το Hospital_DB θα δημιουργηθεί τοπικά στον υπολογιστή σου όταν κάνεις:

Bash
git clone https://github.com/steliosthemelis/Hospital_DB.git

Αυτή τη στιγμή πιθανότατα δεν έχει δημιουργηθεί ακόμα επειδή πριν δεν είχες εγκαταστήσει το Git.

Τώρα που εγκατέστησες Git:

1. Άνοιξε νέο CMD

2. Πήγαινε π.χ. στα Documents

Bash
cd %USERPROFILE%\Documents

3. Κάνε clone το repository

Bash
git clone https://github.com/steliosthemelis/Hospital_DB.git

Αυτό θα δημιουργήσει φάκελο:

Documents/
└── Hospital_DB/

4. Άνοιξέ το στο VS Code

Στο VS Code:

File → Open Folder

και διάλεξε:

Hospital_DB

Τότε θα βλέπεις:

  • db.mwb

  • install.sql

  • και ό,τι άλλο υπάρχει στο GitHub repo.

Από εκεί και πέρα θα δημιουργήσεις:

  • sql/load.sql

  • csv/

  • queries
    κτλ.


Είπες:

I have installed Git and I have done the cloning

Το ChatGPT είπε:

Perfect. Now κάνουμε το σωστό setup για να ξεκινήσεις το load.sql.

1. Άνοιξε το project στο VS Code

Στο VS Code:

File → Open Folder

και διάλεξε τον φάκελο:

Hospital_DB

2. Οργάνωσε το repository σωστά

Η εργασία ζητά συγκεκριμένη δομή φακέλων.

Φτιάξε μέσα στο project:

Hospital_DB/
│
├── sql/
├── csv/
├── diagrams/
├── docs/

3. Μετακίνησε το install.sql

Από root:

install.sql

στο:

sql/install.sql

4. Δημιούργησε το load.sql

Inside sql/:

New File → load.sql

5. Δημιούργησε το πρώτο skeleton

Βάλε μέσα:

SQL
USE hospital_db;

SET FOREIGN_KEY_CHECKS = 0;

-- ICD10
-- KEN
-- Departments
-- Beds
-- Personnel
-- Doctors
-- Nurses
-- Administrative Staff
-- Patients
-- Hospitalizations
-- Prescriptions
-- Lab Exams
-- Procedures
-- Evaluations

SET FOREIGN_KEY_CHECKS = 1;

IMPORTANT:
Το hospital_db πρέπει να είναι το πραγματικό database name από το install.sql.

6. Τώρα το πιο σημαντικό βήμα

Άνοιξε το:

sql/install.sql

και στείλε μου:

  • είτε όλο το αρχείο,

  • είτε screenshots,

  • είτε τα CREATE TABLE statements.

Χωρίς το schema δεν μπορώ να γράψω σωστά inserts/load strategy.

7. Τι θα κάνουμε μετά

Αφού δω το schema, θα σου δώσω:

ακριβή σειρά population

π.χ.

department
→ bed
→ personnel
→ doctor
→ patient
→ hospitalization

ώστε να μην σπάνε τα foreign keys.

8. Θα αποφασίσουμε ποια tables:

MethodUse
INSERT statementsμικροί πίνακες
CSV + LOAD DATAμεγάλοι πίνακες
generated datapatients/personnel

9. Πολύ σημαντικό

ΜΗΝ αρχίσεις random inserts τώρα.

Γιατί:

  • τα queries της εκφώνησης απαιτούν συγκεκριμένα patterns δεδομένων.

Πρέπει να σχεδιάσουμε:

  • data scenarios,

  • distributions,

  • FK consistency,

  • temporal consistency.

10. Άμεσο επόμενο βήμα

Στείλε μου τώρα:

sql/install.sql

ή screenshots από τα CREATE TABLEs.


Είπες:

Ναι το ανοιγω τον Folder. Παρολα αυτα στο this PC δεν υπαρχει το αρχειο

Το ChatGPT είπε:

Αυτό είναι φυσιολογικό αν δεν θυμάσαι ΠΟΥ έκανες το git clone.

Το repository δημιουργείται:

στον φάκελο όπου βρισκόταν το CMD όταν έτρεξες το clone.

Άρα πρέπει να βρούμε πού κατέβηκε.

Εύκολος τρόπος

Άνοιξε CMD και γράψε:

Bash
dir /s /b Hospital_DB

Αυτό θα ψάξει όλο τον υπολογιστή και θα σου δείξει το path.

Πιθανότατα θα είναι κάτι σαν:

C:\Users\YOURNAME\Hospital_DB

ή:

C:\Users\YOURNAME\Documents\Hospital_DB

Εναλλακτικά

Στο CMD γράψε:

Bash
cd

για να δεις πού βρίσκεσαι.

Αν έκανες clone ενώ ήσουν π.χ.:

C:\Users\yourname

τότε ο φάκελος είναι:

C:\Users\yourname\Hospital_DB

Ακόμα πιο εύκολο

Άνοιξε File Explorer και ψάξε πάνω δεξιά:

Hospital_DB

Θα εμφανιστεί ο φάκελος.

Όταν τον βρεις

Στο VS Code:

File → Open Folder

και διάλεξε αυτόν τον φάκελο.


Είπες:


Το ChatGPT είπε:

Perfect — now we know exactly where the repository is.

Your project folder is:

C:\Users\ntoko\Hospital_DB

Now do this:

1. Open VS Code

2. File → Open Folder

Select:

C:\Users\ntoko\Hospital_DB

NOT:

.git

Important.

You must open:

Hospital_DB

the main project folder.

3. After opening

On the left sidebar you should see:

  • db.mwb

  • install.sql

4. Create proper structure

Inside VS Code:

Right click project root →

New Folder → sql

Again:

New Folder → csv

5. Move install.sql

Drag:

install.sql

into:

sql/

So it becomes:

Hospital_DB/sql/install.sql

6. Create load.sql

Right click sql/:

New File → load.sql

7. Put starter skeleton

Inside load.sql:

SQL
USE hospital_db;

SET FOREIGN_KEY_CHECKS = 0;

-- Reference Data
-- ICD10
-- KEN
-- Drugs
-- Active Substances

-- Core Tables
-- Departments
-- Beds
-- Personnel
-- Doctors
-- Nurses
-- Administrative Staff
-- Patients

-- Operational Tables
-- Hospitalizations
-- Triage
-- Prescriptions
-- Lab Exams
-- Procedures
-- Evaluations

SET FOREIGN_KEY_CHECKS = 1;

8. IMPORTANT NEXT STEP

Now I need the schema.

Open:

sql/install.sql

and send me:

  • either the whole file,

  • or screenshots of CREATE TABLEs,

  • or upload the file here.

Then I’ll help you:

  • build the exact insertion order,

  • avoid FK errors,

  • generate realistic data,

  • satisfy the assignment cardinalities,

  • and make sure the queries work.


Είπες:

I am in. I don't wnat to yet organize the file. I just want to start with theload.sql

Το ChatGPT είπε:

Yes, that’s fine.

In VS Code:

  1. Right-click in the left sidebar, inside Hospital_DB

  2. Click New File

  3. Name it exactly:

load.sql

  1. Open load.sql and paste this starter structure:

SQL
-- load.sql
-- Data population script for Hospital_DB

-- Change this if your database has a different name
USE Hospital_DB;

SET FOREIGN_KEY_CHECKS = 0;

-- =========================
-- 1. Reference tables
-- =========================
-- ICD10
-- KEN
-- Insurance providers
-- Medicines
-- Active substances

-- =========================
-- 2. Core hospital tables
-- =========================
-- Departments
-- Beds
-- Personnel
-- Doctors
-- Nurses
-- Administrative staff
-- Patients

-- =========================
-- 3. Operational tables
-- =========================
-- Triage
-- Hospitalizations
-- Lab exams
-- Medical procedures
-- Prescriptions
-- Evaluations

SET FOREIGN_KEY_CHECKS = 1;

Before writing real inserts, open your install.sql and check the exact database name. If it is not Hospital_DB, replace this line:

SQL
USE Hospital_DB;

with the correct one.

To help you write the actual first section, send me the CREATE TABLE statements for your ICD-10 and KEN tables.


Είπες:

Oke now in order to start what do you need?

Το ChatGPT είπε:

To start correctly, I need the actual schema of your tables.

Specifically, send me the CREATE TABLE statements (or screenshots) for:

1. icd10
2. ken

and ideally also:

3. hospitalization

because:

  • ICD10 and KEN are reference tables,

  • but we must know how they connect to hospitalizations.

I need to see:

  • exact table names,

  • column names,

  • primary keys,

  • foreign keys,

  • datatypes,

  • NOT NULL constraints.

For example, I need to know whether your ICD table is:

SQL
CREATE TABLE icd10 (
    code VARCHAR(10) PRIMARY KEY,
    description TEXT
);

or something different.

Same for KEN.

Fastest way

Open install.sql in VS Code and copy-paste here:

SQL
CREATE TABLE ...

for those tables.

Then I’ll give you:

  • the exact first inserts,

  • whether to use INSERT or CSV,

  • how many rows,

  • and how to structure the beginning of load.sql.

IMPORTANT

Do NOT start randomly filling tables yet.

First we determine:

  1. insertion order,

  2. reference data strategy,

  3. FK dependencies,

  4. query requirements.

Otherwise later:

  • constraints break,

  • queries return nothing,

  • and you’ll redo everything.


Είπες:

Do you want the install.sql file ?

Το ChatGPT είπε:

Yes — that’s the best thing you can send.

Upload or paste the install.sql file here.

From that I can:

  • understand the entire schema,

  • determine the correct population order,

  • identify FK dependencies,

  • help you build load.sql,

  • and make sure your data satisfies the assignment queries and constraints.


Είπες:

Do want the raw file or just a copy of it?

Το ChatGPT είπε:

The raw file is better.

Upload the actual install.sql file here. Then I can inspect:

  • all tables,

  • constraints,

  • foreign keys,

  • indexes,

  • datatypes,

  • naming consistency,

and help you construct the load.sql properly from the beginning.


Είπες:

install.sql.txt
Έγγραφο

Το ChatGPT είπε:

Excellent. Now we can work professionally.

Your schema is actually quite solid structurally.

The MOST important thing now is:

population order.

Because your schema is highly interconnected with foreign keys.

FIRST IMPORTANT OBSERVATION

Your database name is:

SQL
USE `mydb`;

NOT:

SQL
Hospital_DB

So in load.sql start with:

SQL
USE mydb;

SECOND IMPORTANT OBSERVATION

You have circular dependencies.

Specifically:

DOCTOR → Department
Department → DOCTOR

because:

  • doctor belongs to departments

  • department has director doctor

This means:

you CANNOT populate everything in naive order.

We must use a staged strategy.

YOUR CORRECT POPULATION STRATEGY

This is the exact order you should follow:

PHASE 1 — Reference Tables

These have almost no dependencies.

Start HERE.

1. ICD-10

ICD-10

2. KEN

KEN

3. Active_Substance

Active_Substance

4. DRUG

DRUG

5. DRUG_has_Active_Substance

DRUG_has_Active_Substance

6. ROOM

ROOM

These are ideal first tables.

PHASE 2 — Core Personnel

7. PERSONNEL

This is the parent table of almost everything.

You should create:

  • doctors

  • nurses

  • admin staff

HERE first.

NOT in subtypes yet.

PHASE 3 — DOCTOR / DEPARTMENT circular dependency

This is tricky.

We solve it like professionals.

Step A

Insert SOME doctors first.

BUT:
your schema currently says:

SQL
Supervisor_AMKA NOT NULL

This is problematic because:

  • Directors should not have supervisors according to assignment.

Your schema currently violates assignment semantics.

Still, for now:

  • create 2–3 “top directors”

  • temporarily make them supervise themselves OR each other.

Not ideal logically, but necessary unless teammate changes constraint.

Step B

Create departments.

Now directors exist.

Step C

Populate:

DOCTOR_has_Department

PHASE 4 — Remaining Personnel

8. Nurse

9. Administrative_Staff

Now departments exist, so FK works.

PHASE 5 — Physical Hospital

10. BEDS

Depends on Department.

PHASE 6 — Patients

11. Patient

You need:

~200 patients

according to assignment.

12. Emergency_Contact

13. Patient_Allergy

Depends on:

  • Patient

  • Active_Substance

PHASE 7 — Operational Tables

14. Shift

15. Shift staffing tables

  • DOCTOR_has_Shift

  • Nurse_has_Shift

  • Administrative_Staff_has_Shift

PHASE 8 — Hospitalizations

16. Hospitalization

VERY important table.

Depends on:

  • Patient

  • Beds

  • ICD10

  • KEN

You need:

~500 hospitalizations

PHASE 9 — Triage

17. Triage

Depends on:

  • Nurse

  • Patient

  • Hospitalization

PHASE 10 — Medical Activity

18. LAB_EXAMS

19. Medical_Procedures

20. Procedure_Team

PHASE 11 — Prescriptions

21. Perscription

VERY important for queries.

Needs:

  • doctor

  • patient

  • drug

  • hospitalization

You need:

~300 prescriptions

PHASE 12 — Evaluations

22. Evaluation

23. DoctorEvaluation

ONLY for completed hospitalizations.

WHAT YOU SHOULD DO TODAY

Do NOT attempt full load.sql immediately.

Start ONLY with:

1. ICD10
2. KEN
3. ROOM
4. Active_Substance
5. DRUG
6. DRUG_has_Active_Substance

This is the cleanest starting point.

VERY IMPORTANT WARNING

Your table:

SQL
ICD-10

is dangerous because:

  • table names with -
    require backticks EVERYWHERE.

You must write:

SQL
INSERT INTO `ICD-10`

NOT:

SQL
INSERT INTO ICD-10

Otherwise SQL interprets:

ICD minus 10

MY RECOMMENDATION

Eventually rename:

ICD-10

to:

ICD10

before project becomes huge.

Same for:

Perscription

(spelling typo)

because these WILL create pain later.

YOUR NEXT BEST STEP

Start your load.sql with:

SQL
USE mydb;

SET FOREIGN_KEY_CHECKS = 0;

Then create section:

SQL
-- =====================================
-- ICD10
-- =====================================

Now:
tell me which table you want to populate FIRST:

  • ICD10

  • KEN

  • DRUG

  • ROOM

and I’ll help you build the actual inserts/import strategy professionally.


Είπες:

Can I ask something. If I do a chnage in VScode will that also change my teammates work?

Το ChatGPT είπε:

Not immediately.

This is the MOST important GitHub concept.

When you change something in VS Code, the changes are initially:

ONLY on your computer.

Your teammate does NOT see them yet.

The lifecycle is:

Edit file locally
    ↓
Commit
    ↓
Push
    ↓
Teammate pulls

Example

You edit:

load.sql

inside VS Code.

At this point:
✅ only YOU see the changes.

Then you do:

Bash
git add .
git commit -m "Added ICD10 data"

Still:
✅ only YOU have the changes locally.

ONLY after:

Bash
git push

the changes go to GitHub.

NOW:

  • teammate can download them.

Even then:

Your teammate STILL does not automatically get the changes.

They must do:

Bash
git pull

to receive them.

So the actual synchronization model is:

ActionWho sees changes?
Edit in VS Codeonly you
git commitonly you
git pushGitHub updated
teammate git pullteammate gets updates

VERY IMPORTANT

You are safe to experiment locally.

You can:

  • edit,

  • test,

  • break things,

  • undo changes,

WITHOUT affecting teammate until:

Bash
git push

BEST PRACTICE FOR YOU

Since you handle data population:

Work mainly on:

load.sql
csv/

while teammate works on:

install.sql
constraints
indexes

This minimizes conflicts.

IMPORTANT WARNING

If BOTH edit:

same file
same lines

then Git may create:

merge conflicts

Avoid this.

Your safest workflow

Before starting work:

Bash
git pull

Work in VS Code

After testing:

Bash
git add .
git commit -m "Added KEN data"
git push

That’s the entire collaboration cycle.


Είπες:

Lets do the phase 1 in our population strategy

Το ChatGPT είπε:

Ωραία, πάμε στο Phase 1 πρακτικά και με ασφάλεια: θα φτιάξουμε πρώτα μόνο τα reference tables, ώστε να μην μπλέξουμε ακόμα με προσωπικό/νοσηλείες/βάρδιες.

Ναι. Phase 1 = γεμίζουμε πρώτα πίνακες που δεν εξαρτώνται από άλλους πίνακες.

Στο δικό σας schema αυτοί είναι:

1. `ICD-10`
2. KEN
3. ROOM
4. Active_Substance
5. DRUG
6. DRUG_has_Active_Substance

Το database name σας είναι mydb, άρα το load.sql πρέπει να ξεκινά έτσι: 

SQL
USE mydb;

SET FOREIGN_KEY_CHECKS = 0;

-- =========================
-- PHASE 1: REFERENCE DATA
-- =========================

1. ICD-10

Επειδή ο πίνακας λέγεται ICD-10, ΠΡΕΠΕΙ πάντα να βάζεις backticks:

SQL
INSERT INTO `ICD-10` (`ICD-10`, `Description`) VALUES
('I10', 'Essential hypertension'),
('E11', 'Type 2 diabetes mellitus'),
('J18', 'Pneumonia'),
('K35', 'Acute appendicitis'),
('S72', 'Fracture of femur'),
('I21', 'Acute myocardial infarction'),
('N39', 'Urinary tract infection'),
('C50', 'Breast cancer'),
('F32', 'Depressive episode'),
('Z00', 'General examination');

2. KEN

SQL
INSERT INTO KEN (ken_code, base_cost, avg_stay_days, extra_day_rate) VALUES
('KEN001', 1200.00, 3, 180.00),
('KEN002', 2500.00, 5, 250.00),
('KEN003', 4200.00, 7, 350.00),
('KEN004', 900.00, 2, 120.00),
('KEN005', 6000.00, 10, 500.00),
('KEN006', 1800.00, 4, 200.00),
('KEN007', 3200.00, 6, 280.00),
('KEN008', 750.00, 1, 100.00);

3. ROOM

Χρειάζεστε τουλάχιστον 10 χειρουργεία/αίθουσες. Η εκφώνηση ζητά ενδεικτικά 10 χειρουργεία/αίθουσες και 150 ιατρικές πράξεις. 

SQL
INSERT INTO ROOM (room_id, room_name, room_type, capacity) VALUES
(1, 'Operating Room 1', 'Surgery', 1),
(2, 'Operating Room 2', 'Surgery', 1),
(3, 'Operating Room 3', 'Surgery', 1),
(4, 'Operating Room 4', 'Surgery', 1),
(5, 'Operating Room 5', 'Surgery', 1),
(6, 'Procedure Room 1', 'Intervention', 1),
(7, 'Procedure Room 2', 'Intervention', 1),
(8, 'Imaging Room 1', 'Diagnostic', 1),
(9, 'Endoscopy Room 1', 'Diagnostic', 1),
(10, 'Emergency Procedure Room', 'Emergency', 2);

4. Active_Substance

SQL
INSERT INTO Active_Substance (substance_id, substance_name) VALUES
(1, 'Paracetamol'),
(2, 'Ibuprofen'),
(3, 'Amoxicillin'),
(4, 'Clavulanic Acid'),
(5, 'Metformin'),
(6, 'Insulin'),
(7, 'Atorvastatin'),
(8, 'Omeprazole'),
(9, 'Aspirin'),
(10, 'Ceftriaxone'),
(11, 'Furosemide'),
(12, 'Salbutamol');

5. DRUG

SQL
INSERT INTO DRUG
(product_name, route_of_admin, auth_country, auth_holder, master_file_loc, phv_email, phv_phone)
VALUES
('Panadol 500mg', 'Oral', 'Greece', 'GSK', 'EU/EMA/PANADOL', 'phv@gsk.com', '+302100000001'),
('Brufen 400mg', 'Oral', 'Greece', 'Abbott', 'EU/EMA/BRUFEN', 'phv@abbott.com', '+302100000002'),
('Augmentin 875/125mg', 'Oral', 'Greece', 'GSK', 'EU/EMA/AUGMENTIN', 'phv@gsk.com', '+302100000003'),
('Glucophage 500mg', 'Oral', 'Greece', 'Merck', 'EU/EMA/GLUCOPHAGE', 'phv@merck.com', '+302100000004'),
('Lantus', 'Injection', 'Germany', 'Sanofi', 'EU/EMA/LANTUS', 'phv@sanofi.com', '+302100000005'),
('Lipitor 20mg', 'Oral', 'Ireland', 'Pfizer', 'EU/EMA/LIPITOR', 'phv@pfizer.com', '+302100000006'),
('Losec 20mg', 'Oral', 'Sweden', 'AstraZeneca', 'EU/EMA/LOSEC', 'phv@az.com', '+302100000007'),
('Aspirin 100mg', 'Oral', 'Germany', 'Bayer', 'EU/EMA/ASPIRIN', 'phv@bayer.com', '+302100000008'),
('Rocephin 1g', 'Injection', 'Switzerland', 'Roche', 'EU/EMA/ROCEPHIN', 'phv@roche.com', '+302100000009');

6. DRUG_has_Active_Substance

Εδώ συνδέεις φάρμακα με δραστικές ουσίες.

SQL
INSERT INTO DRUG_has_Active_Substance
(DRUG_drug_id, Active_Substance_substance_id)
VALUES
(1, 1),
(2, 2),
(3, 3),
(3, 4),
(4, 5),
(5, 6),
(6, 7),
(7, 8),
(8, 9),
(9, 10);

Στο τέλος του load.sql βάζεις:

SQL
SET FOREIGN_KEY_CHECKS = 1;

Προς το παρόν, το αρχείο σου πρέπει να έχει περίπου αυτή τη μορφή:

SQL
USE mydb;

SET FOREIGN_KEY_CHECKS = 0;

-- ICD10 inserts
-- KEN inserts
-- ROOM inserts
-- Active_Substance inserts
-- DRUG inserts
-- DRUG_has_Active_Substance inserts

SET FOREIGN_KEY_CHECKS = 1;

Μετά κάνεις save στο VS Code.

Για test από CMD:

Bash
mysql -u root -p < load.sql

Αν είσαι μέσα στον φάκελο Hospital_DB. Αν όχι, τρέξε με πλήρες path.


Είπες:

Oke lets start with ICD10. I will give you the proper EXEL file containing all the codes

Το ChatGPT είπε:

Τέλεια. Ανέβασε εδώ το Excel αρχείο με τα ICD-10.

Μόλις το δω, θα σου πω:

  • ποια columns χρειαζόμαστε,

  • αν ταιριάζουν με τον πίνακά σας ICD-10,

  • πώς να το καθαρίσουμε αν χρειάζεται,

  • και θα σου ετοιμάσω το σωστό κομμάτι για το load.sql.

Θυμίζω ότι ο πίνακάς σας είναι:

SQL
`ICD-10` (
  `ICD-10` VARCHAR(10),
  `Description` VARCHAR(45)
)

Άρα χρειαζόμαστε ουσιαστικά δύο στήλες:

code, description

Είπες:

4

Αδυναμία εμφάνισης απεικόνισης

Το ChatGPT είπε:

Perfect. This is exactly the kind of file the assignment expects you to use for reference data.

Now we’ll do this professionally.

STEP 1 — Put the file inside the project

Inside your project folder create:

csv/

and place the ICD file there.

Example:

Hospital_DB/
│
├── load.sql
├── install.sql
└── csv/
    └── icd10.xls

You can rename the file to something simpler like:

icd10.xls

STEP 2 — Open the Excel file

I need you to check:

  • what the column names are,

  • and which columns contain:

    • ICD code

    • description

Usually these files contain something like:

ICD CodeDescription
I10Hypertension

STEP 3 — VERY IMPORTANT PROBLEM

Your table currently is:

SQL
CREATE TABLE `ICD-10` (
  `ICD-10` VARCHAR(10),
  `Description` VARCHAR(45)
)

The problem is:

SQL
VARCHAR(45)

is TOO SMALL for real ICD descriptions.

Many ICD descriptions exceed 45 characters.

You should tell your teammate to change this BEFORE population.

Recommended:

SQL
`Description` VARCHAR(255)

or even:

SQL
TEXT

Otherwise inserts will fail or truncate data.

This is a very important schema correction.

STEP 4 — Best import strategy

DO NOT manually write thousands of INSERTS.

Instead:

Convert Excel → CSV

In Excel:

File → Save As → CSV UTF-8

Save as:

icd10.csv

inside:

csv/

STEP 5 — Then we’ll use LOAD DATA

Inside load.sql we’ll eventually write something like:

SQL
LOAD DATA LOCAL INFILE 'csv/icd10.csv'
INTO TABLE `ICD-10`
FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','
ENCLOSED BY '"'
LINES TERMINATED BY '\n'
IGNORE 1 ROWS
(`ICD-10`, `Description`);

This imports thousands of rows instantly.

IMPORTANT NEXT STEP

Now I need you to do ONE thing:

Open the Excel file and send me:

  1. screenshot of the first rows,
    OR

  2. the exact column names.

Then I’ll tell you:

  • whether cleaning is needed,

  • whether UTF-8 issues exist,

  • and the exact final LOAD DATA statement.


Είπες:

4

Αδυναμία εμφάνισης απεικόνισης

Το ChatGPT είπε:

I received the file, but I cannot directly inspect .xls spreadsheets unless you either:

  1. upload a screenshot of the first rows, OR

  2. open it in Excel and tell me:

    • which column contains the ICD code,

    • which column contains the description.

I specifically need to see something like:

ColumnMeaning
AICD code
BDescription

because then I can give you:

  • the exact CSV cleanup steps,

  • the correct LOAD DATA statement,

  • and ensure it matches your table:

SQL
`ICD-10` (
  `ICD-10`,
  `Description`
)

Also IMPORTANT:
before importing, your teammate should change:

SQL
Description VARCHAR(45)

to:

SQL
Description VARCHAR(255)

otherwise many ICD descriptions will be cut off or fail.


Είπες:

There seems to be a problem in my ability to upload the execl file to you

Το ChatGPT είπε:

That’s okay — .xls files are sometimes problematic in chat systems.

Do this instead:

EASIEST OPTION

Open the Excel file and send me:

  • a screenshot of the first 10–20 rows.

That’s enough.

I mainly need to identify:

  • the ICD code column,

  • the description column,

  • whether there are extra headers/merged cells.

OR EVEN EASIER

Tell me:

Column A = ?
Column B = ?
Column C = ?

For example:

A = ICD code
B = Description

That alone may be enough.

MOST IMPORTANT THING RIGHT NOW

Before importing ANYTHING:

Your teammate should modify this in install.sql:

FROM:

SQL
`Description` VARCHAR(45)

TO:

SQL
`Description` VARCHAR(255)

because ICD descriptions are often much longer than 45 chars.

Otherwise imports will break/truncate.


Είπες:

Can you help me do this on my own ?

Το ChatGPT είπε:

Yes — and honestly this is the right approach. You should learn how to handle dataset imports yourself because you’ll repeat the same workflow for:

  • ICD10,

  • KEN,

  • drugs,

  • procedures,

  • maybe even patients.

So let’s do it step-by-step yourself.

GOAL

We want to import ICD10 data into:

SQL
`ICD-10` (
  `ICD-10`,
  `Description`
)

using:

Excel → CSV → LOAD DATA INFILE

STEP 1 — Open the Excel file

Open:

4.2 Κωδικοί ICD-10 15-12-2011.xls

STEP 2 — Find the useful columns

You are looking ONLY for:

  • ICD code

  • ICD description

Usually something like:

AB
I10Hypertension

Ignore everything else.

STEP 3 — Clean the file

Delete:

  • titles,

  • merged headers,

  • empty rows,

  • notes,

  • formatting junk.

You want a clean table like:

ICD10Description
I10Essential hypertension
E11Type 2 diabetes mellitus

STEP 4 — IMPORTANT SCHEMA FIX

Open:

install.sql

Find:

SQL
`Description` VARCHAR(45)

Change to:

SQL
`Description` VARCHAR(255)

Otherwise many rows will fail.

STEP 5 — Save as CSV

In Excel:

File → Save As

Choose:

CSV UTF-8 (*.csv)

Name it:

icd10.csv

Save inside your project folder.

Example:

Hospital_DB/csv/icd10.csv

STEP 6 — Add import code to load.sql

Inside load.sql:

SQL
-- =====================================
-- ICD10 IMPORT
-- =====================================

LOAD DATA LOCAL INFILE 'C:/Users/ntoko/Hospital_DB/csv/icd10.csv'
INTO TABLE `ICD-10`
FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','
ENCLOSED BY '"'
LINES TERMINATED BY '\n'
IGNORE 1 ROWS
(`ICD-10`, `Description`);

IMPORTANT:

  • use / not \

  • use FULL PATH

STEP 7 — Test the import

First run:

Bash
mysql -u root -p

Then:

SQL
USE mydb;

Then:

SQL
source C:/Users/ntoko/Hospital_DB/load.sql

STEP 8 — Verify

Run:

SQL
SELECT * FROM `ICD-10` LIMIT 10;

If rows appear:
✅ import successful.

COMMON PROBLEMS

Problem:

Data too long for column

Solution:

Increase VARCHAR size

Problem:

Incorrect column count

Solution:

CSV contains extra columns

Problem:

NULL values

Solution:

Remove empty rows

IMPORTANT PROFESSIONAL ADVICE

Do NOT import:

all raw government spreadsheet garbage directly

Always:

clean → simplify → import

That is standard database engineering practice.


Είπες:


Το ChatGPT είπε:

Choose:

CSV (Οριοθετημένο με κόμματα)

NOT:

  • CSV (MS-DOS)

  • CSV (Macintosh)

Just the normal CSV option.

Then:

  1. Save it as:

icd10.csv

  1. Put it inside:

Hospital_DB/csv/

  1. After saving:

  • open the CSV once quickly,

  • verify Greek characters/descriptions look normal.

Then we’ll proceed to:

LOAD DATA LOCAL INFILE

and import it into MySQL professionally.


Είπες:


Το ChatGPT είπε:

Press:

Ναι

This warning is normal.

Excel is simply telling you:

“CSV only stores raw table data, not Excel formatting/macros/sheets.”

That is EXACTLY what we want for database import.


Είπες:

All the descriptions have turned into this

Το ChatGPT είπε:

Good catch. This is an encoding problem — very common with Greek datasets.

The CSV was saved in a Windows encoding and Excel reopened it incorrectly.

DO NOT panic. Your original .xls file is still fine.

BEST SOLUTION

Instead of:

CSV (comma separated)

save again as:

CSV UTF-8

If available.

If you do NOT see "CSV UTF-8"

Then use:

Κείμενο Unicode

This preserves Greek correctly.

IMPORTANT

The corrupted ????? happens ONLY because:

  • Excel reopened the CSV using wrong encoding.

Your original Excel data is NOT destroyed.

Recommended workflow now

1.

Close the broken CSV WITHOUT saving.

2.

Reopen the original .xls.

3.

Save As →

Choose:

Κείμενο Unicode

OR:

CSV UTF-8

(if available)

Why this matters

Your database uses UTF-8:

SQL
DEFAULT CHARACTER SET utf8

So Greek text can work perfectly.

The issue is only:

Excel export encoding

not MySQL.


Είπες:

After that what should I do?

Το ChatGPT είπε:

After saving as:

CSV UTF-8

OR:

Κείμενο Unicode

do this carefully:

STEP 1 — Verify the file

Open the newly saved file once.

Check:

  • Greek descriptions display correctly,

  • no ?????.

If Greek appears correctly:
✅ continue.

STEP 2 — Put file inside your project

Example:

C:/Users/ntoko/Hospital_DB/csv/icd10.csv

or:

C:/Users/ntoko/Hospital_DB/csv/icd10.txt

depending on format.

STEP 3 — Modify load.sql

Inside load.sql, create section:

SQL
-- =====================================
-- ICD10 IMPORT
-- =====================================

STEP 4 — Add import statement

IF you saved as CSV UTF-8:

SQL
LOAD DATA LOCAL INFILE 'C:/Users/ntoko/Hospital_DB/csv/icd10.csv'
INTO TABLE `ICD-10`
CHARACTER SET utf8
FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','
ENCLOSED BY '"'
LINES TERMINATED BY '\n'
IGNORE 1 ROWS
(`ICD-10`, `Description`);

IMPORTANT

Before running this:
your teammate MUST change:

SQL
Description VARCHAR(45)

to:

SQL
Description VARCHAR(255)

inside install.sql.

Otherwise MANY ICD rows will fail.

This is critical.

STEP 5 — Run install.sql again

Because schema changed.

Inside MariaDB:

SQL
DROP DATABASE mydb;

then rerun:

Bash
mysql -u root -p < install.sql

This recreates corrected tables.

STEP 6 — Run load.sql

Then:

Bash
mysql -u root -p --local-infile=1 < load.sql

IMPORTANT:
--local-infile=1

because MySQL blocks local imports otherwise.

STEP 7 — Verify import

Inside MariaDB:

SQL
USE mydb;

SELECT * FROM `ICD-10` LIMIT 10;

If Greek descriptions appear correctly:
✅ success.

VERY IMPORTANT WARNING

Your table name:

SQL
`ICD-10`

ALWAYS needs backticks.

Never forget this:

SQL
SELECT * FROM `ICD-10`;

NOT:

SQL
SELECT * FROM ICD-10;

because SQL interprets that as:

ICD minus 10

Είπες:

?

Το ChatGPT είπε:

Press:

Yes

This is just VS Code detecting:

  • you are using MySQL/MariaDB syntax,

  • not Microsoft SQL Server syntax.

Disabling SQL Server IntelliSense is the correct choice for your project.


Είπες:


Το ChatGPT είπε:

That means your database mydb has not been created yet.

This is completely fine.

Now do this instead:

1. Exit MariaDB

SQL
exit

2. From normal CMD run install.sql

Go to your project folder:

Bash
cd C:\Users\ntoko\Hospital_DB

Then run:

Bash
mysql -u root -p < install.sql

Press Enter for password if empty.

This will:

  • create mydb,

  • create all tables,

  • apply constraints.

3. Then enter MariaDB again

Bash
mysql -u root -p

4. Verify database exists

SQL
SHOW DATABASES;

You should now see:

mydb

5. Use database

SQL
USE mydb;

6. Verify tables

SQL
SHOW TABLES;

If tables appear:
✅ schema installed correctly.

Then we continue with ICD10 import.


Είπες:


Το ChatGPT είπε:

This happens because MySQL is not in your Windows PATH.

No problem.

Use the FULL path to MySQL.

Run:

Bash
C:\xampp\mysql\bin\mysql -u root -p < install.sql

If install.sql is inside:

C:\Users\ntoko\Hospital_DB

then run it from that folder exactly as you are now.

So:

Bash
cd C:\Users\ntoko\Hospital_DB

then:

Bash
C:\xampp\mysql\bin\mysql -u root -p < install.sql

Press Enter for password if empty.


Είπες:


Το ChatGPT είπε:

You are in the wrong folder now.

The file:

install.sql

exists inside:

C:\Users\ntoko\Hospital_DB

but your CMD is currently at:

C:\Users\ntoko>

So first go into the project folder:

Bash
cd C:\Users\ntoko\Hospital_DB

THEN run:

Bash
C:\xampp\mysql\bin\mysql -u root -p < install.sql

Now Windows will find the file correctly.


Είπες:

And after that?

Το ChatGPT είπε:

After running:

Bash
C:\xampp\mysql\bin\mysql -u root -p < install.sql

this should happen:

  • database mydb gets created,

  • all tables get created,

  • constraints/FKs get installed.

Then do this

1. Enter MariaDB

Bash
C:\xampp\mysql\bin\mysql -u root -p

2. Check databases

SQL
SHOW DATABASES;

You should now see:

mydb

3. Select database

SQL
USE mydb;

4. Verify tables exist

SQL
SHOW TABLES;

You should see:

  • PERSONNEL

  • DOCTOR

  • Patient

  • Hospitalization

  • ICD-10

  • etc.

5. THEN we test your load.sql

Exit MariaDB:

SQL
exit

Go back to CMD.

Inside:

C:\Users\ntoko\Hospital_DB

run:

Bash
C:\xampp\mysql\bin\mysql --local-infile=1 -u root -p < load.sql

This executes your population script.

6. Then verify ICD10 import

Enter MariaDB again:

Bash
C:\xampp\mysql\bin\mysql -u root -p

then:

SQL
USE mydb;

then:

SQL
SELECT * FROM `ICD-10` LIMIT 10;

If rows appear:
✅ your first real dataset import succeeded.

That will be your first fully working Phase 1 population table.


Είπες:

This is because we used the csv file instead of the txt

Το ChatGPT είπε:

Exactly. Good diagnosis.

The CSV encoding corrupted the Greek characters.

Your best solution now is:

DO NOT use the CSV file.

Instead:

  • reopen the original .xls,

  • save as:

Κείμενο Unicode

This preserves Greek properly.

Then

Save it as something simple:

icd10.txt

inside:

C:\Users\ntoko\Hospital_DB\csv\

Then modify load.sql

Use:

SQL
LOAD DATA LOCAL INFILE 'C:/Users/ntoko/Hospital_DB/csv/icd10.txt'
INTO TABLE `ICD-10`
CHARACTER SET utf8
FIELDS TERMINATED BY '\t'
LINES TERMINATED BY '\r\n'
IGNORE 1 ROWS
(`ICD-10`, `Description`);

IMPORTANT:
Unicode text files from Excel are usually:

  • tab-separated,

  • NOT comma-separated.

That’s why we use:

SQL
FIELDS TERMINATED BY '\t'

Then rerun

Bash
C:\xampp\mysql\bin\mysql --local-infile=1 -u root -p < load.sql

Then verify

Inside MariaDB:

SQL
